message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  Optimizing vaccination to reduce
                  <jats:bold>COVID-19</jats:bold>
                  death remains a challenge. A new method,
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>Gompertzian Analysis</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  , examines numbers of infectious disease cases and deaths, by age, on log graphs, capturing
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Lethality</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  (Deaths/Cases) by the
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>Gompertz Mortality Equation</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  .
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>Gompertzian Analysis</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  revealed that each of the first 4
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>Vaccination Events</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  (primary and boosters) led to a ∼1/3
                  <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                  reduction in
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Lethality</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  . These vaccination reductions in
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Lethality</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  were cumulative, persistent, and undiminished by variants, while vaccination’s impact on
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Infectivity</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  (Cases/Population) was fleeting. Primary vaccination and 3 boosters gave an ∼85% reduction in
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Lethality</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  , with projections suggesting ∼68% fewer deaths (∼267,000 to ∼85,000). Projections also suggest that 6 boosters may offer a ∼96% reduction in
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Lethality</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  , to the familiar level of influenza, with a ∼91% reduction in
                  <jats:bold>COVID-19</jats:bold>
                  deaths, to ∼25,000, fewer than automobile deaths.
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>Gompertzian Analysis</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  provides rational vaccination guidelines by age.
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>Gompertzian Analysis</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  points to a strategy molded by multiple vaccinations reducing
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Lethality</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  , which is persistent, rather than focusing on reducing
                  <jats:bold>
                    COVID-19
                    <jats:italic>Infectivity</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  , which is fleeting. Such a strategy, based on accumulating the necessary number of vaccinations (∼7), and possibly no more, would accept vaccination’s limited ability to prevent infection in exchange for its power to prevent death.
                </jats:p>
title: A New Vaccination Assessment Method and Strategy for COVID-19
